facade_colour: brown brick upper floor (barely visible), dark-painted commercial ground floor with striped awning

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through an early-twentieth-century vernacular-style two-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as part of a row with the adjacent properties at 30-32 St. Andrew Street, the property retains building features that reflect vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. The nineteenth-century structure has retained its front gable, and brick clad west elevation with second storey window opening and sloped roofline. Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the addition has retained its brick facade above the second storey with cornice and window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 372. 34 St. Andrew Street 61 Kensington Avenue 61-1/2 Kensington Avenue 63 Kensington Avenue 65 Kensington Avenue 67 Kensington Avenue 69 Kensington Avenue 36 St. Andrew Street 198 DCH | FO :D 1 & 3 Wales Avenue (L-R) 1 & 3 WALES AVENUE

photo_observations.storefront_description: Full-width storefront with accordion security gates, multiple bays of glazing. Kensington Leather Fashion signage. Orange-and-white striped retractable awning.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: entire facade converted to commercial row, original Victorian structure almost completely obscured, security gates, graffiti
photo_observations.notes: Correctly a Converted House-form per HCD. Commercial ground floor IS expected for this typology. Front gable and brick west elevation from HCD statement still present but concealed by awning. matches_hcd corrected from false to true.

facade_description: 67 Kensington Ave presents a row-house facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, row typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 0, 0 window bays from lower to upper levels, using storefront, and 2 principal entrances. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: The property has value as a House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through an early-twentieth-century vernacular-style two-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, the property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as part of a row with the adjacent properties at 30-32 St. Andrew Street, the property retains building features that reflect vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. The nineteenth-century structure has retained its front gable, and brick clad west elevation with second storey window opening and sloped roofline. Constructed between 1924 and 1930, the addition has retained its brick facade above the second storey with cornice and window openings with segmentally arched brick voussoirs. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area. Summary: Reference Primary Address Entrance Address(es) Number 372. 34 St. Andrew Street 61 Kensington Avenue 61-1/2 Kensington Avenue 63 Kensington Avenue 65 Kensington Avenue 67 Kensington Avenue 69 Kensington Avenue 36 St. Andrew Street 198 DCH | FO :D 1 & 3 Wales Avenue (L-R) 1 & 3 WALES AVENUE
